figures.caption: Fig. 2: Buoyancy plots for Rayleigh-Bénard convection at different values of 𝑅𝑎 and 𝑃𝑟 = 1.0. The top row shows a late-time snapshot at 𝑅𝑎 = 106, where the flow exhibits organized large-scale convective rolls with relatively smooth temperature gradients. The distinction between these values is important because it directly impacts the feasibility of reduced-order modeling: the slow singular-value decay observed at 𝑅𝑎 = 1010 (Section 3.1) reflects this increased dynamical complexity.

Fig. 3 Illustration of the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and its low-rank truncation. (a) The full SVD decomposes a snapshot matrix X ∈ R𝑛×𝑚 into the product U𝚺V𝑇 , where U contains the left singular vectors (spatial modes), 𝚺 is a diagonal matrix of singular values in decreasing order, and V𝑇 contains the right singular vectors (temporal coefficients). (b) The truncated SVD retains only the first 𝑟 modes, yielding the best rank-𝑟 approximation of X in the Frobenius and spectral norms (Eckart– Young–Mirsky theorem). The discarded singular values and their associated modes represent fine-scale or low-energy features of the data.

Fig. 4: Visualization of POD spatial modes for the 2D lock-exchange problem. From top to bottom: a representative snapshot of the concentration field at an intermediate time, the time-averaged concentration (mean field), and the first four normalized POD modes (columns of U𝑟 ). Red and blue regions indicate positive and negative mode values, respectively. The progressive increase in spatial oscillation frequency from Mode 1 to Mode 4 reflects the hierarchical structure of the SVD: lower modes capture largescale coherent structures, while higher modes represent increasingly fine-scale features. Discarding higher-order modes in the truncated SVD thus removes high-frequency spatial content.

Fig. 6 DMD reconstruction performance for the 2D lockexchange problem at different truncation ranks 𝑟 . (a) Temporal evolution of the relative 2-norm error 𝜂: the error tracks the physical dynamics, peaking during highly transient phases and decreasing as the flow stabilizes. Increasing 𝑟 consistently reduces the reconstruction error. (b–c) Quantities of interest (front position and sediment mass) are well conserved across all approximations, confirming the physical consistency of the DMD reconstruction. Taken from [7]
Fig. 7: Singular values, 𝜅 and relative Frobenius norm between the truncated approximation and the ground truth data for the Rayleigh-Bénard dataset.
Fig. 8 Results and mesh for the first 8 length units of the domain at 𝑡 = 10 time units, from [9].

Fig. 9: Geometry and initial conditions for the 3D lock-exchange simulation (top) and Sediment concentration snapshot at t = 10 time units (bottom). The figure, from [8], shows the sediment concentration 3D volume rendering.

Fig. 10: Singular values for the tested cases. The dashed line represents the number of 𝑟 = 250 singular values and vectors selected for the DMD basis. The key observation is that the lossless and high-fidelity lossy (10−6) compressed datasets closely track the uncompressed baseline throughout the retained spectrum. The more aggressive lossy compression (10−3) introduces a plateau in the singular values that begins before the truncation threshold, indicating that some physically relevant modal information has been altered by the compression. This spectral analysis provides a practical diagnostic for assessing whether a given compression level is compatible with a target truncation rank. From [8].

Fig. 11: DMD eigenvalues for the 3D lock-exchange simulation plotted in the complex plane. (a–b) Comparison of Cases A (raw), B (lossless), and C (lossy 10−6), showing near-identical spectral distributions. (c–d) Comparison of Cases A and D (lossy 10−3), showing significant eigenvalue displacement due to aggressive compression. The right panels zoom into the boxed region near the unit circle. Eigenvalues appear as complex conjugate pairs because the snapshot data is real-valued. The proximity of eigenvalues to the unit circle indicates nearly neutral stability of the corresponding modes.
Fig. 12 Reconstruction error and QoIs between DMD approximations using compressed data and ground truth (raw data). Taken from [8]

Fig. 13: Schematic of a PINN used to solve a 2D density-driven gravity flow problem. The neural network 𝑁𝑁 (𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑡; 𝜃) takes spatio-temporal coordinates as input and outputs approximations of the velocity components (?̃?, ?̃?), pressure (𝑝), and concentration (𝑐). The PDE residuals {𝑒𝑘}4 𝑘=1 corresponding to the continuity, momentum, and transport equations (Eqs. (10)–(12)) are computed via automatic differentiation and penalized at collocation points. For this problem, the settling velocity 𝑢𝑠 = 0. The loss function L is the weighted sum of the PDE loss evaluated at the collocation points and the data mismatch, with optional 𝐿2-regularization for parameter estimation tasks.
Fig. 20: 𝐿2 error for multiple runs using the datasets in Table 2.
Fig. 21: SVD-reconstruction of the concentration using different mode-truncation criteria. Snapshots at 𝑡 = 6𝑠.
Fig. 22: Evolution of the collocation points for two different sampling techniques.
Fig. 24: Snapshots of the reconstruction of the 𝑢−velocity.
Fig. 25: Concentration (left), 𝑢−velocity (middle), and 𝑣−velocity (right) errors over time.
Fig. 26: Evolution of the losses, errors, and dynamic loss weights for different collocation points sampling schemes.
Table 4: Hyper-parameters for Grashof Identification.
Fig. 29: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) architecture. The encoder maps the highdimensional input field into a latent representation parameterized by the mean 𝝁 and standard deviation 𝝈.

Fig. 30: Comparison of reconstructed fields and error metrics across different Rayleigh numbers and 𝛽 penalty constraints. Top figures are results for 𝑅𝑎 = 106 and bottom results are for 𝑅𝑎 = 1010. Left, center and right plots are, respectively, ground truth, reconstruction using 𝛽 = 0.00100 and 𝛽 = 0.00001.
Fig. 31 Field approximations for ReLoBRaLo (top), SoftAdapt (middle) and 𝛽 evolution over training time. The ratio between the weights can oscillate up to 2 orders of magnitude.
